hard.next_step: Schedule a detailed RFP-workflow demo with Lester, Tim and Alechia (evaluation scoring, functional assessments, supplier onboarding, Sage integration, Merlin Intake via Teams), send focused discovery questions on Sage ingestion and RFP weighting first, and run the first-week-July in-person visit to align stakeholders.
hard.ais_why:
* Explicit customer excitement on the 26-May call: notes record "Excitement about agentic AI and guided intake for advanced procurement automation" (Lester Ash) and "Recognition of AI-driven data governance and ESG insights as critical capabilities" (Tim Damons).
* Positive reaction to Zycus AI specifically: "Merlin guided intake and agentic AI highlighted versus legacy systems; prospect reacted positively," with interest in a Merlin/Teams chat interface for self-service procurement requests.
* Tempered by readiness risk, not AI doubt: their "data is a mess," and past tool adoption stalled on budget; next steps are a detailed demo and a July in-person visit - real momentum but no POC/budget yet, hence Medium confidence.
hard.competitor: Do Nothing (manual sourcing via Sage + Onetrust)

north_star_verdict.headline: Discovery is re-exploring source-to-contract automation after two prior budget-driven pauses, and the 26 May discovery call landed well: Lester Ash was keen to hear more, and Alechia Foster and Tim Damons pulled for a deeper RFP-workflow demo. But the deal has gone quiet for roughly six weeks with no buyer touch since 26 May, three Zycus commitments (the demo, the discovery questions, and John Woodcock's first-week-July visit) are undelivered or unconfirmed, and the economic buyer in finance is unmapped. Lester framed the effort as exploratory, 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there,' so the real threat is do-nothing, not a rival. Restarting cadence this week and getting a phased, ROI-anchored business case in front of finance is what keeps the 30 Nov date credible.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 2026-05-26, qualified 2026-05-28 (fast, 2 days). About 39 days in Qualified stage (2026-05-28 to 2026-07-06). 147 days to the 2026-11-30 close, under 5 months, which is aggressive for a Qualified-stage deal with no formal evaluation, no pricing, amount at 0, and remaining milestones (deeper demo, RFP validation, commercials, finance sign-off, MSA/SOW, InfoSec, contracting). No forward slip in field history: the close moved from 2026-05-26 to 2026-11-30 on 2026-06-04 as a planned extension, not a slip. Current pace: 41 days since the only buyer touch (2026-05-26) with no logged activity, so momentum has thinned; not yet cold (under the 60-day Off Track threshold). To hold 30 Nov, cadence must restart within 7 days, the deeper demo and RFP validation land within 30 days, and finance/EB engagement plus commercials begin within 60 days; otherwise the date likely slips into Q1 2027.
evidence: Last verified buyer touch was the 2026-05-26 discovery call with Lester Ash (CPO), Tim Damons and Alechia Foster; no logged Salesforce activity since (LastActivityDate 2026-05-26), roughly six weeks of silence.

The 26 May call landed positively: Alechia Foster said 'I would definitely like to see a further demo on this, especially in terms of the RFP process, how involved is it, is it possible to do full evaluations, functional scoring, weightings,' and Lester Ash said 'sounds good, thanks, just a bit keen to hear some more.'

Budget is the primary vulnerability: Lester Ash recounted two prior pauses, 'the budgets that have been proposed are way out of our appetite, and we're gonna put it on hold,' and a later look with 'similar results and feedback... the budgets are out of filter' (2026-05-26).

The economic buyer sits in finance and is unmapped: Tim Damons said 'when it comes to purchasing our own tools... procurement always have to sell our value proposition and our business cases,' and 'the price tags didn't quite match what the business perceived to be of value' (2026-05-26). No finance contact role exists.

Do-nothing is the real threat, not a rival: Discovery runs sourcing 'pretty much manually,' uses Onetrust for onboarding ('not very automated, not very user friendly, still manual'), and Lester Ash said they are 'not actively stopping, but just trying to understand what's out there' (2026-05-26).

Readiness risk: Lester Ash said 'our data is a mess at the moment... where the tool is and where we still need to go is quite a long way,' and Tim Damons flagged low procurement maturity and the need to fix the data foundation before feeding the platform (2026-05-26).

metrics.narrative: Discovery has clearly articulated the operational pain but has not quantified it. Lester Ash described the state on 2026-05-26: 'It's mainly manual. We use Sage as our financial tool, and for sourcing, pretty much manually. We've got Onetrust that we use as an onboarding tool for new service providers, but it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual.' Tim Damons framed the value as data visibility and ESG governance: 'ESG is becoming a very important aspect of procurement, understanding exactly what you're buying, when you're buying it,' and admitted 'our data is a mess.' No cost, headcount, cycle-time, savings or ROI figure has been put on any of this, which is exactly why the historical business case failed against price. Quantifying the manual-sourcing and onboarding cost is the ranked move that unlocks the finance conversation.
sources: Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'It's mainly manual... for sourcing, pretty much manually. We've got Onetrust... it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'ESG is becoming a very important aspect of procurement, understanding exactly what you're buying, when you're buying it.'
AIS_Why__c: past tool adoption stalled on budget; no POC/budget yet.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is named or engaged. The Salesforce contact roles cover procurement only: Lester Ash (Group CPO, Decision Maker), Tim Damons and Alechia Foster (Evaluators). Budget authority visibly lives in finance and the board, not procurement: Tim Damons said on 2026-05-26 that for their own tools 'procurement always have to sell our value proposition and our business cases,' and that historically 'the price tags didn't quite match what the business perceived to be of value.' Twice before, an unnamed finance/board budget owner shelved Zycus on cost. Identifying and engaging that finance sponsor, and getting a phased ROI case in front of them, is the single highest-leverage gap to close before any commercial step.
sources: Salesforce OpportunityContactRole: Lester Ash (Decision Maker), Tim Damons (Evaluator), Alechia Foster (Evaluator, primary); no Economic Buyer / Executive Sponsor role.
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'procurement always have to sell our value proposition and our business cases... the price tags didn't quite match what the business perceived to be of value.'
Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'the budgets that have been proposed are way out of our appetite, and we're gonna put it on hold.'

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer stated concrete evaluation criteria on 2026-05-26. Alechia Foster wants proof the RFP process runs fully in-system: 'is it possible to do full evaluations, functional scoring, weightings... so that we can be sure that it's not half a process where we still have to do half of it outside of the system.' Tim Damons set a data-foundation criterion: how Zycus ingests Sage extracts and builds procurement taxonomy from cost centres and GL accounts that today have 'no taxonomy' and 'a lot of free text.' Across both, phased deployment and fast ROI are gating criteria given the budget history, and ESG/data-governance visibility is a stated priority. These are confirmed asks but not yet formalised into an RFP or scoring matrix, so criteria are known but not documented.
sources: Alechia Foster, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'full evaluations, functional scoring, weightings... not half a process where we still have to do half of it outside of the system.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'how do you feed that initial dataset into Zycus... build your foundation to then feed the platform and Merlin.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'ESG is becoming a very important aspect of procurement.'

decision_process.narrative: Read from the call itself, Discovery is running an informal market-scan, not a formal RFP yet. Lester Ash (Group CPO) framed it as 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there,' and brought his two direct reports, Tim Damons and Alechia Foster, to evaluate. The agreed next step is a deeper demo scoped by the three of them: Tim confirmed 'yes, the three of us' should attend, and Lester offered his PA to schedule. Downstream, any purchase must pass a finance/board business-case gate that has twice rejected Zycus on price. So the near process is procurement-led evaluation, but the real decision gate is finance funding, which is not yet in the process. No formal timeline or compelling event has been named by the buyer.
sources: Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'we are not, by any means, actively stopping, but we're just trying to understand what's out there.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'Yes. The three of us.'
Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'I'm also looking, PA that you can reach out just to schedule the meeting.'

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have surfaced, which is appropriate for a Qualified-stage exploration with amount at 0. There has been no discussion of MSA, SOW, Order Form, legal, procurement channel or InfoSec, and no partner or systems integrator is involved. The only forcing element is historical, not contractual: the finance/board budget approval that gated the two prior evaluations. John Woodcock previewed the eventual mechanic on 2026-05-26, 'we sign up to the milestones with you in the SOW,' but that is a future step. Visibility here is created only once a quantified business case moves the deal to commercials and finance engages, so paper process is a genuine but stage-appropriate gap.
sources: John Woodcock, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'when you're presenting a business case to the board and we sign up to the milestones with you in the SOW.'
Salesforce: Stage Qualified, Amount 0, no legal/InfoSec/SOW activity in scope.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is concrete and owned. Sourcing is 'pretty much manually' (Lester Ash), supplier onboarding via Onetrust is 'not very automated, not very user friendly, still manual' (Lester Ash), and the underlying data is unusable for procurement: Tim Damons said cost centres and GL accounts have 'no taxonomy... a lot of free text' and that 'our data is a mess.' The consequence is no reliable spend visibility or ESG/governance view, on a 32-year-old business that is 'still quite young' in procurement maturity (Tim Damons). The pain owners are Lester Ash (Group CPO, accountable for shifting the procurement dial) and Tim Damons (owns IT/professional-services/marketing categories). This is confirmed, articulated pain, only missing a number.
sources: Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'It's mainly manual... Onetrust... it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'there's no taxonomy around it... it's a lot of free text... our data is a mess.'
SF Customer_Business_Problem__c: procurement heavily manual across P2P, supplier onboarding and RFP execution.

champion.narrative: Lester Ash (Group CPO, Decision Maker) is the strongest relationship: he knows Zycus well, met John Woodcock at an awards dinner, and closed the call 'keen to hear some more.' Tim Damons is the most vocal advocate, articulating the value story and the data-foundation vision himself. But this is a developing, not yet secured, champion: neither has introduced finance, driven urgency, or moved internally since 26 May, and Lester's own posture is exploratory ('not actively shopping'). What has developed is genuine engagement and a pull for the next demo; what is missing is advocacy into the finance budget owner and a compelling event. With six weeks of silence, the champion is at risk of cooling, so securing Lester as an active sponsor ranks ahead of any commercial step.
sources: Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'sounds good... just a bit keen to hear some more.'
Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'the value of a tool like Zycus is really around how it can be used to enhance your procurement experience.'
Salesforce LastActivityDate 2026-05-26: no buyer-facing follow-up logged in ~41 days.

competition.narrative: This is effectively a sole-source exploration whose real competitor is do-nothing. Discovery runs sourcing manually on Sage with Onetrust for onboarding, and has twice shelved automation on cost. The named alternatives are historical, not active: Tim Damons said they have 'done a number of RFPs over the years looking at all the different tools... whether that's Ivalua or Jaggaer, and Coupa,' but none is in play now. A local South African vendor they backed to build a competitive tool failed ('unfortunately, that didn't work out... we're back at square one'), which is why Zycus is being re-engaged. The wedge is Merlin guided intake, agentic automation, fast implementation via 100+ APIs into Sage, and a phased land-and-expand entry that beats the budget wall; the way we win is a quantified, phased ROI case that keeps do-nothing from winning by inertia.
sources: Tim Damons, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'done a number of RFPs over the years... whether that's Ivalua or Jaggaer, and Coupa.'
Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'one of the companies that pitched was a local company... unfortunately, that didn't work out... we're back at square one.'
Lester Ash, discovery call 2026-05-26: 'not actively stopping, but just trying to understand what's out there.'

deal_movement.summary: Since qualification on 2026-05-28 the deal has not moved: the only buyer touch is the 2026-05-26 discovery call, and there has been no logged activity in the ~41 days since. The 2026-06-04 close-date change (2026-05-26 to 2026-11-30) was a planned extension by John Woodcock, not a slip. Momentum is thinning while three Zycus commitments remain undelivered.

items.change: Discovery re-engaged after two historical budget pauses and a failed local-vendor investment; positive 26 May discovery call, buyer pulled for a deeper RFP demo.
Stage moved Initial Interest to Qualified (by Rashi Varma).
Close date extended 2026-05-26 to 2026-11-30 (planned, by John Woodcock), not a slip.
No buyer-facing activity logged since 26 May; demo, discovery questions and July visit still open.

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is do-nothing / status quo: Discovery has run sourcing manually on Sage with Onetrust for onboarding for years and twice shelved automation on cost, and Lester Ash is 'just trying to understand what's out there' (2026-05-26). No rival is actively in play. Named alternatives (Ivalua, Jaggaer, Coupa) are historical RFP references with no current activity; a local South African vendor Discovery invested in failed and left them 'back at square one' (faded); Onetrust is the incumbent onboarding tool being displaced. Zycus is effectively sole-source, so the deal is won or lost against inertia, not a competitor.

competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the manual-sourcing and onboarding cost and land a phased, fast-ROI first module so inertia is the more expensive option.

competitors.quote: We've got Onetrust that we use as an onboarding tool for new service providers, but it's not very automated, it's not very user friendly, it's still manual
competitors.how_we_win: Show Zycus iSupplier automating onboarding end-to-end versus Onetrust's manual secure-file exchange.

competitors.name: Local South African vendor (unnamed)
competitors.quote: one of the companies that pitched was a local company which we wanted to support... unfortunately, that didn't work out, and a year and a half or two later we're back at square one
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus's proven multi-org deployments (Tiger Brands, Woolworths in-region) against the failed local build.

competitors.how_we_win: Not in play now; if revived, lead with Merlin agentic intake and faster implementation ROI.

competitors.how_we_win: Not in play now; differentiate on guided intake and Sage integration if it resurfaces.

competitors.quote: done a number of RFPs over the years... whether that's Ivalua or Jaggaer, and Coupa
competitors.how_we_win: Not in play now; counter on total cost and phased entry given Discovery's price sensitivity.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Discovery's stated expectations centre on a fully in-system RFP evaluation process, a workable Sage data-ingestion and taxonomy foundation, phased affordable deployment, and ESG/data-governance visibility. Zycus is aligned on capability but exposed on the historically decisive dimension, price/budget.

best_practice_check.summary: Win-strategy levers for an early, price-sensitive re-exploration where do-nothing is the real competitor and finance owns the budget.
flags: Lead with a phased land-and-expand entry (one high-ROI module, not the full suite) to break the historical budget wall that paused Zycus twice.
Multi-thread into finance now: procurement has twice failed to fund a tool, so identify and engage the budget owner before any pricing conversation.
Leverage Discovery's existing Zycus UK footprint ('a utility UK team using the tool') as a licensing and reference lever to de-risk the business case.

items.risk: Cooling after ~41 days of silence; has not engaged finance or driven urgency, and could shelve the effort a third time if the price/appetite gap re-emerges.

What has developed is genuine interest and a pull for the next demo; what is missing is active sponsorship into the budget owner and a compelling event. With ~41 days of silence the champion is at risk of cooling.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus's Merlin guided intake and agentic AI landed well on 26 May: John Woodcock walked the intake-tower and 700-agent orchestration story, and notes record excitement about agentic AI and interest in a Merlin/Teams self-service interface. The AI story is well positioned; the constraint is data readiness and budget, not AI appeal.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 7.0 / Moderate is corroborated by the call: explicit excitement about agentic AI and guided intake, and recognition of AI-driven data governance and ESG as critical, tempered by 'our data is a mess' and no budget/POC yet. The AI appetite is real but gated by data-foundation readiness.

items.detail: Discovery paused Zycus twice on price ('way out of our appetite') and no budget is approved this time; amount is 0.
Economic buyer sits in finance and is unmapped; procurement must sell the business case to finance to fund any tool.
Do-nothing risk: exploratory posture with no compelling event; Discovery can keep managing sourcing manually and never decide.
'Our data is a mess' and low procurement maturity mean data-foundation work could stall adoption even after a purchase.

critical_signals.text: The 26 May discovery call landed well: Lester Ash was keen to hear more and Alechia Foster and Tim Damons pulled for a deeper RFP-workflow demo.
This is effectively sole-source against do-nothing: Discovery runs sourcing manually and has no rival actively in play after a local vendor investment failed.
Discovery shelved Zycus twice because the price was 'way out of our appetite', and no budget is confirmed while finance stays unengaged.
The buyer needs proof the RFP evaluation, scoring and weighting run fully in-system and that Zycus can ingest Sage data and build taxonomy.

scope_change.detail: Scope unchanged versus the prior record; buyer prefers a phased land-and-expand entry, which is a sequencing preference, not a scope reduction.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Discovery is re-exploring source-to-contract automation after two prior budget-driven pauses; the 26 May discovery landed well and they pulled for a deeper RFP demo, but no budget is confirmed, the finance economic buyer is unmapped, and they have gone quiet for roughly six weeks, so the real threat is do-nothing.

win_position.text: Zycus is the only tool Discovery is currently evaluating and the 26 May discovery landed well, but this is early re-exploration with no budget and no finance sponsor.
Discovery paused Zycus twice because pricing was 'way out of our appetite' (Lester Ash, 26 May) and no budget is approved this time.
The economic buyer sits in finance and is unmapped; Tim Damons admits procurement must 'sell the business case' to fund any tool (26 May).

deal_momentum.text: Alechia Foster and Tim Damons asked for a deeper RFP-workflow demo and Lester Ash said he was 'keen to hear more' (26 May), a genuine customer pull.
No buyer touch since the 26 May call, roughly six weeks of silence, with the demo, discovery questions and July visit all still undelivered.

customer_commitment.text: Lester Ash framed it as exploratory, 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there' (26 May), with no compelling event or timeline.
Discovery brought its Group CPO, a senior procurement manager and the bid/sourcing lead to the first call, a multi-threaded procurement team that owns the process.

deal_risk.text: Do-nothing is the real threat: Discovery has run sourcing manually on Sage and Onetrust for years and twice shelved automation on cost, so it can simply keep the status quo.
'Our data is a mess' (Lester Ash, 26 May) and low procurement maturity mean readiness and data-foundation work could stall adoption even if they buy.

concrete_dates.evidence: The buyer named no dates; only Zycus proposed the July visit (26 May).

budget_confirmed.evidence: Two prior pauses on price; no budget approved and finance unmapped (26 May).

customer_engaged_recent.evidence: No buyer touch in ~41 days since the 26 May call.

customer_preference.evidence: Reacted positively to Merlin guided intake and pulled for a deeper RFP-specific demo, but Lester Ash said 'not actively shopping, just understanding what's out there' (26 May) - positive, not decisive.

business_case.evidence: Strong qualitative pain (manual sourcing, non-automated Onetrust onboarding, messy data, ESG visibility gap) but no cost, savings or ROI figure quantified (26 May).
